content.text: What It Is

A Tech Radar is a curated, opinionated snapshot of the technology landscape relevant to an engineering organisation. It places each technology, tool, platform, or technique into one of four rings — Adopt, Trial, Assess, or Hold — arranged across quadrants such as Languages and Frameworks, Platforms, Techniques, and Tools. The radar is not static: entries move between rings as the organisation's experience accumulates, as providers change their offering, and as the broader ecosystem evolves.

Tech Radar governance cadence is the scheduled, repeating process that keeps the radar accurate and actionable. Without a cadence, radars are published once and then silently stagnate. For AI-intensive teams in particular, where model capabilities, API pricing, and tool ecosystems shift monthly, a stale radar is worse than no radar: it actively misleads teams into using technologies that should have moved to Hold, or missing tools that have matured to Adopt.

The cadence answers five operational questions: who proposes new entries and ring changes, who reviews and votes, when reviews happen, how promoted or deprecated entries connect to Architectural Decision Records, and how the published radar reaches every engineer in the organisation.
Why It Matters

Agentic AI systems consume a uniquely fast-moving stack: LLM APIs, embedding models, vector stores, orchestration frameworks, evaluation tooling, and observability platforms all evolve faster than traditional infrastructure. A technique that was Assess-ring six months ago (say, structured output via constrained decoding) may now be Adopt-ring because every major provider has standardised the API. Conversely, an orchestration library that was Trial-ring may have accumulated breaking changes and an abandoned maintainer, warranting an immediate Hold.

Without formal governance cadence, decisions about which technologies to use are made informally, inconsistently across teams, and without institutional memory. One squad uses a framework on Hold while another has already migrated to the Adopt-ring replacement. A quarterly cadence surfaces these inconsistencies, creates a forcing function for explicit decisions, and produces a governance artefact that satisfies audit requirements for AI system procurement in regulated industries.

The integration with ADRs matters because every ring change is, by definition, a significant architectural decision. Linking each radar promotion or demotion to an ADR creates a traceable chain: the radar tells you what ring a technology is in, the ADR tells you why it moved and who approved the move, and the ADR review lifecycle tells you whether that rationale is still considered valid.
Benefits
Consistent technology choices.

All squads draw from the same Adopt ring rather than independently evaluating the same candidates. This reduces evaluation fatigue and prevents incompatible technology choices in services that must integrate.
Explicit retirement of technical debt.
The Hold ring with a grace-period window creates a structured path to migrate away from technologies that are no longer appropriate, rather than letting them accumulate indefinitely.
AI-team alignment.

AI engineering teams often evaluate experimental models and tooling in parallel with platform teams who manage infrastructure and security. A shared cadence surfaces conflicts early — before an experimental tool reaches production.
Audit and compliance evidence.

The combination of radar entry history, ADR links, and a versioned radar repository provides a timestamped record of technology decisions that satisfies AI governance frameworks and enterprise procurement policies.
Reduced cognitive load.

Engineers spend less time evaluating whether to use a technology if the radar clearly signals its ring. A strong Adopt-ring recommendation short-circuits individual research for well-understood choices.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Quarterly radar governance cycle: Q1 Collect & Propose, Q2 Review & Score, Q3 Publish & Broadcast, Q4 Monitor & Retire, with a central platform team coordinating.
attrs.title: Tech Radar Quarterly Governance Cadence

content.text: The diagram shows the four-quarter cycle. Q1 opens proposal intake: any engineer submits a new entry or ring-change request via pull request using the entry template. Q2 is the scoring window: the review panel meets to evaluate each proposal against the ring criteria and votes. Q3 is publication: the approved radar is generated, published to the organisation wiki, and communicated via engineering all-hands and team newsletters. Q4 is the monitoring and retirement phase: usage metrics for Hold-ring entries are reviewed, grace periods expire, and entries with no active champions are formally deprecated.
Quarterly Review Process
Q1 — Collect and Propose

The proposal window opens on the first working day of Q1 and closes three weeks before the Q2 review session. Any engineer may propose a new radar entry or a ring change for an existing entry by opening a pull request against the radar repository. The PR must use the standard entry template, which requires: a concise title, the proposed ring and quadrant, a rationale paragraph, links to evaluation artefacts (spike writeups, benchmarks, PoC repositories), and, for ring promotions, evidence of production usage or a completed trial.

The platform team triages incoming proposals during the proposal window. Proposals that lack required fields are returned to the author with specific feedback. Proposals for emergency Hold designations — for example, a critical unpatched CVE in a Trial-ring library — bypass the quarterly queue and go directly to an emergency review convened within 48 hours.
Q2 — Review and Score

The review panel convenes for a structured scoring session. Each proposal is scored on four dimensions: technical maturity (is the technology stable enough for its proposed ring?), team fit (does the organisation have the skills to operate it?), risk profile (security, compliance, and vendor dependency), and strategic alignment (does adopting this technology support the organisation's AI engineering roadmap?). Scores are numeric, 1–5 per dimension, and the weighted average maps to a ring recommendation.

Panel members vote on the final ring placement. A simple majority of voting members determines the outcome; ties are broken by the platform architect. Votes and rationale are recorded in the PR comments for audit purposes. The review session also evaluates existing entries for demotion: any Adopt or Trial entry with no champion, significant unresolved issues, or a superior replacement in the radar is proposed for Hold or retirement.
Q3 — Publish and Broadcast

After the review session, the platform team merges approved entries, regenerates the radar visualisation (typically using a tool like Thoughtworks' radar baked generator or a custom static site generator), and publishes the new radar to the organisation's engineering portal. For every entry whose ring changed, a corresponding ADR is created or updated, linking the radar decision to the architectural rationale. The publication is announced in the engineering newsletter, with a summary of net-new Adopt entries, demotions to Hold, and retirements.
Q4 — Monitor and Retire

Q4 focuses on operational hygiene. The platform team reviews usage telemetry for Hold-ring entries — are teams still adding new dependencies on tools they should be migrating away from? Entries that have exceeded the grace period with no active migration plan trigger a conversation with the owning squad. Full retirement (removal from the radar, not just movement to Hold) happens when no production system depends on the entry and the grace period has elapsed.
Participation Structure
The governance body for Tech Radar reviews comprises three mandatory participant categories and two advisory categories.
Platform Architect (required, voting).
Chairs the review session. Has final tie-breaking authority. Ensures the radar reflects the organisation's infrastructure strategy and is responsible for the ADR linkage requirement.
Tech Leads (required, voting, minimum 2).
Represent the engineering teams that will be consuming the radar. Bring evidence from real project work about the practical usability of proposed entries.
AI Team Representative (required, voting).

Brings the perspective of LLM-intensive workloads: model evaluation, prompt engineering tooling, evaluation frameworks, and AI observability. This role is distinct from a general tech lead because AI tooling moves on a different cadence than backend infrastructure.
Security Engineer (optional, advisory).
Reviews proposals for CVEs, dependency risk, and data handling compliance. Does not vote but can veto a Trial-to-Adopt promotion on security grounds, subject to platform architect override.
Product Engineering Lead (optional, advisory).
Provides product velocity context: does moving this library to Hold create blockers for near-term product milestones? Advisory input influences scheduling of grace periods.
Radar Entry Ring Definitions
Adopt.
The organisation endorses this technology for production use. Teams should default to Adopt-ring choices. Deviation requires justification in a service-level ADR.
Trial.
Worth pursuing in a defined pilot. Teams may use with written approval from their platform architect. Must file an evaluation report within one radar cycle.
Assess.
Interesting enough to warrant a spike or proof of concept. Not yet suitable for production. An individual engineer or small working group can explore without formal approval.
Hold.
Do not start new work with this technology. Existing usages must file a migration plan within the grace period (default: 90 days). No new dependencies may be added.
Proposal and Promotion Workflow
Engineer identifies a technology worth adding or changing. They check the current radar to confirm it is not already in the target ring.
Engineer opens a pull request against the radar repository using the standard entry template. The PR title follows the convention: [PROPOSE] <entry-id>: <title> -> <target-ring>.
Platform team triage: within 5 business days, the proposal is either accepted into the Q2 review queue, returned for missing information, or fast-tracked to emergency review.
If accepted into the queue: the proposal waits for the Q2 review session. The engineer may be asked to present their evaluation evidence for 5 minutes at the session.
Review panel scores the proposal. Voting members record their score and rationale in the PR comments.
If approved: the PR is merged, the radar entry YAML is updated, and an ADR is created or linked. If rejected: the PR is closed with a documented rationale and the entry moves to Hold or is removed.
Q3 publication: the radar is regenerated and published. The engineer who proposed the entry is notified of the outcome.

Q4 follow-up: for Trial entries that were promoted, the platform team schedules an evaluation checkpoint for the following Q2 session to assess whether the entry should be promoted to Adopt or demoted.
Integration with Architectural Decision Records

Every ring change in the radar must reference at least one ADR. This requirement is enforced by a CI lint step that validates radar entry YAML: any entry whose ring field differs from the previous radar version must have at least one non-empty entry in the adr_refs list. The lint step fails the PR if this condition is not met.

The ADR linked to a radar ring change follows the standard ADR format but includes two additional sections. The Radar Context section records the previous ring, the proposed ring, and the review session date. The Evidence section records links to the evaluation artefacts that informed the decision: benchmark results, spike repositories, incident post-mortems, or security assessments.

This bidirectional linkage — the radar entry links to the ADR, and the ADR's frontmatter records the radar entry ID — means that engineers can navigate from either direction. Starting from the radar: click through to understand why a technology is in its current ring. Starting from the ADR: see which radar entry the decision governs and trace the full history of ring changes.
YAML Radar Governance Policy

# radar-governance-policy.yaml
# Defines the governance rules for the organisation's Tech Radar.

radar:
  cadence:
    review_frequency: quarterly          # Q1 propose / Q2 review / Q3 publish / Q4 retire
    publication_window_days: 14          # how long after Q3 opens before radar is published
    emergency_update_allowed: true       # critical security hold entries can skip the queue

  participants:
    required:
      - role: platform_architect
        min_count: 1
        voting: true
      - role: tech_lead
        min_count: 2
        voting: true
      - role: ai_team_representative
        min_count: 1
        voting: true
    optional:
      - role: security_engineer
        voting: false                    # advisory only
      - role: product_engineering_lead
        voting: false

  rings:
    - id: adopt
      description: >
        Proven, recommended for production use. Teams should default
        to Adopt-ring entries unless there is a specific justification.
    - id: trial
      description: >
        Worth pursuing in a limited pilot. Teams may use with approval
        from their platform architect.
    - id: assess
      description: >
        Worth exploring to understand its impact. Assign a spike or PoC;
        do not use in production without escalation.
    - id: hold
      description: >
        Do not start new work with this technology. Existing usages
        should file a migration plan within the next radar cycle.

  entry_lifecycle:
    proposal:
      method: pull_request
      template: docs/radar/entry-template.yaml
      required_fields: [title, ring, quadrant, rationale, links]
      review_sla_business_days: 10

    promotion_rules:
      assess_to_trial:
        requires: [spike_results_doc, platform_architect_approval]
      trial_to_adopt:
        requires: [production_usage_evidence, security_review, two_voting_approvals]
      any_to_hold:
        requires: [rationale, migration_path_doc, one_voting_approval]

    deprecation:
      triggers:
        - provider_eol_announced
        - security_critical_cve_unfixed
        - cost_increase_exceeds_threshold_pct: 300
        - superseded_by_adopt_entry
      grace_period_days: 90             # entry stays visible with DEPRECATED badge

  adr_integration:
    required_on_ring_change: true       # every ring promotion/demotion must reference an ADR
    adr_directory: docs/adr/
    link_field: adr_refs                # field in radar entry YAML that lists ADR IDs
Python Radar Diff Generator

The following tool compares two radar YAML snapshots and generates a human-readable changelog. Run it in CI as part of the radar PR validation to surface ring changes and ensure every change is documented.
Common Anti-Patterns
Publishing without broadcasting.
Teams do not read the radar if it is not proactively communicated. A quiet wiki update reaches nobody. The Q3 broadcast step is not optional.
Hold without migration path.
Moving an entry to Hold is meaningless if the affected teams have no clear path to migrate. Every Hold designation must be accompanied by a named Adopt or Trial alternative.
Radar as individual authority.
One engineer with strong opinions should not be able to move an entry to Hold in retaliation for a technology choice they dislike. The voting quorum requirement prevents this.
Skipping ADR linkage for minor ring changes.
All ring changes are architectural decisions regardless of direction. The ADR requirement must apply to demotions as well as promotions.
Radar covering too many quadrants for the team size.
A five-person team maintaining a 200-entry radar across six quadrants cannot keep it current. Scope the radar to the technologies your teams actually evaluate and use.
Key Takeaways
A quarterly cadence with four distinct phases — propose, review, publish, monitor — keeps the radar accurate without consuming disproportionate engineering time.
The review panel must include AI team representation; AI tooling evolves on a different cadence than traditional backend infrastructure and requires domain-specific evaluation criteria.
Every ring change requires a linked ADR. This creates a traceable chain from the radar entry to the architectural rationale to the approval record.
Hold designations must include a migration path and a grace period. Without these, Hold is a label that gets ignored.
Emergency Hold designations for critical CVEs bypass the quarterly queue and must be actionable within 48 hours of disclosure.
The radar diff generator in CI surfaces ring changes in every PR, making it impossible to accidentally update the radar without documenting the change.
